quotes.quote: Often you do research in one language and you implement it in another.

We saw a clear need for a next-generation business intelligence / visual analytics product.
The amount of planning and ETL work needed to make an existing BI system "fit" is frequently prohibitive.
We made an early decision to design a new architecture to enable the BI process to be more agile and iterative.
The system we built, code-named Badger, delivered an interactive analytics experience that exceeded our own expectations in performance and truly delighted our users.
It was clear to us that what's holding back next-generation BI/visual analytics is much more on the data management and systems side.
So the question came down to: continue building a standalone data product and duke it out in the marketplace, or use our systems expertise to accelerate the rising tide that will lift all ships.
On this front, we're really looking forward to working together; there is a lot of work to be done to enable developers and data scientists to get value out of their data faster and more intuitively.

Realistically, the performance of ingesting data into pandas via Arrow should be significantly faster than reading a CSV (being binary and columnar).

The dirty secret with Feather's performance is that neither Hadley or I spent much effort on performance optimization.
Due to Feather's design (using Arrow's columnar memory layout and simple metadata using Google's Flatbuffers library), there was simply no way for it to be slow.
By "metadata-free", I mean that in storing data in these formats, you lose type information (for example: factor levels) that may be impossible to recover.
When you read the files, you have to perform expensive type inference to "guess" the data types of columns.

In both the Python and R communities, we've spent an extraordinary amount of time writing fast code for parsing and doing type inference on all of the bizarre delimited text files generated in the real world.

I often tell people that one of the things that made pandas successful early on was that `pandas.read_csv` usually just worked and was fairly fast, and that wasn't true of any other Python CSV readers at the time.
This is why Apache Arrow is so important: it gives the community a common table / data frame memory layout on which we can collaborate more easily.
Feather's priorities in order have been: 1. Interoperable metadata and a shared memory layout 2. Shared code 3. Performance.

Mechanical keyboards have become a big business the last 5 years or so, with clackity-clack Cherry MX key switches becoming all the rage amongst programmers and gamers alike.

They are expensive keyboards (at US $300 to $400, depending on the model and where you buy it), but given that they can last a decade or more, properly taken care of, you can think of it as an investment in your professional productivity and health.
The big changes in the Advantage2 are: the function keys are now proper mechanical Cherry ML switches rather than the spongier capacitative ones in the older models.
An internal USB-based "V-Drive" enabling you to edit configuration files (layouts and macros) and install firmware updates on the keyboard programmability and firmware updates from your computer.
The top speed of 9 is 20 times faster, a blistering 250 characters per second (around 3000 words per minute).
Any macros created onboard are similarly reflected in the text file, so you can make tweaks to more complicated macros from a text editor (so-called "direct editing").
For Kinesis users who take advantage of customization and macros, the Advantage2 is a very nice upgrade for its improved programmability and the better feel of the function keys.

If you are a new or potential user, and you can afford the investment (of money and time to learn the contoured keyboard shape), it should be worth your consideration over the increasingly long list of mechanical keyboards now available.

Arrow is an in-memory columnar container ideal as a transport layer to and from Parquet.
I'm planning to merge the Feather implementation into the Arrow codebase, which will enable me to provide better performance and new features to Feather users.
In Apache Arrow, an in-memory columnar array collection representing a chunk of a table is called a record batch.
In the streaming format, we send a series of messages: the schema followed by one or more record batches.
Since this data is in memory, reading back Arrow record batches is a zero-copy operation.
As the streaming chunksize grows smaller, the cost to reconstruct a contiguous columnar pandas DataFrame increases because of cache-inefficient memory access patterns.
This is an effective throughput of 7.75 GB/s to reconstruct a 1GB DataFrame from 1024 1MB chunks.
The performance degrades significantly from 256K to 64K chunks.
In the current iteration of the format, the data is not being compressed at all, so the in-memory and on-the-wire size are about the same.
Streaming columnar data can be an efficient way to transmit large datasets to columnar analytics tools like pandas using small chunks.

With a dataset that occupies 1 gigabyte (1024 MB) in a pandas.DataFrame, with Snappy compression and dictionary encoding, it occupies an amazing 1.436 MB, small enough to fit on an old-school floppy disk.
Without dictionary encoding, it occupies 44.4 MB.
For low entropy data, decompression and decoding becomes CPU-bound.
Because we are doing all the work in C++, we are not burdened by the concurrency issues of the GIL and thus can achieve a significant speed boost.
With 4 threads, the performance reading into pandas breaks through an amazing 4 GB/s.
This is much faster than Feather format or other alternatives I've seen.
The Python bindings are a lightweight wrapper on top of the underlying libarrow and libparquet C++ libraries.

Zero-copy data access was the biggest thing; you need to be able to memory map complex tables to make accessing 1 terabyte of data on disk as fast and easy as 1 megabyte.
I strongly feel that Arrow is a key technology for the next generation of data science tools.
In Arrow, each string is right next to the previous one in memory, so you can scan all of the data in a column of strings without any cache misses.
pandas’s lack of a C or Cython API for data frames has been another big problem over time.
Dask makes it easy to read a directory of CSV files by running pandas.read_csv in parallel and then running a groupby operation on the entire dataset.
dask.dataframe does not solve pandas’s inherent performance and memory use problems, but it spreads them out across multiple processes.
Feather files are Arrow memory on disk (and thus support zero-copy access), but have more limited metadata.

Moving Feather development to the Arrow codebase has made the implementation substantially simpler, and Feather users immediately benefit from any performance improvements or general Arrow-related development work.
Perhaps amazingly, Feather 0.4.0 based on pyarrow 0.7.1 is much faster than Feather 0.3.1.
Feather 0.3.1 can read a 50-column DataFrame at about 1.6 GB/s while Feather 0.4.0 ... can read at about 2.9 GB/s, a reduction in runtime of over 40%.
Until R bindings for Apache Arrow ship, it will be difficult to innovate on Feather's feature set.
I'm excited to engage with the R community on this work and look forward to delivering more Apache Arrow-based IO and computation tools to both the R and Python communities.
Arrow is not competing with Parquet and ORC.
Arrow is an ephemeral, runtime in-memory representation that can be processed without any overhead on a per array-cell basis.
What Arrow provides a Parquet or ORC columnar storage user is a standardized in-memory data structure to place decoded data.
If you don't use something standardized, in many cases you will have to roll your own "proprietary", non-portable data structures.
One of the most game-changing aspects of Apache Arrow, is its role in data access and data movement.
That we have not implemented these compression schemes is not a function of our ignorance, but rather our available time and engineering resources.
We must walk before we can run.
As more pieces of Arrow fall into place, and more integrations in other projects get built, things are going to get very exciting for all involved.
Funding open source software development is a complicated subject.
In my experience, open source projects feature two dominant archetypal modes: innovation and maintenance.
OSS maintainers, which are often volunteers, routinely “burn out” under the strain of supporting burgeoning user bases who sometimes take a project’s existence and maintenance for granted.
If we had refused to build pandas unless we raised enough money to pay for our rent and families’ cost of living, the project likely would not be what it is today.

As Apache Arrow has gotten off the ground over the last few years, it has become apparent that the problems we are tackling are much larger in scope than the interests of a single organization or even programming language.
I envision a portable, community-standard “data science runtime” that can be utilized for processing native Arrow-based data frames in just about any programming language.
The reality is that Hadley and I think the “language wars” are stupid when the real problem we are solving is human user interface design for data analysis.
We are only now at the beginning of a long journey ahead of us to advance the state of the art in data science tools.
The best code is the code that you don't have to write.
There's 15 people who are essentially making these three open-source projects that we all depend on work, which is sort of terrifying.

If developers burn out, it can make a project significantly weaker.
A lot of open-source maintainers carry so much project history and knowledge in their heads that it would take an individual months or years to be effective.
The two most significant sources of funding for open-source, the number one is self-funding, so no funding.

One of the reasons I like community-led projects is that there are fewer single points of failure.

So big O doesn't help you, it really comes down to your memory access patterns.
The idea of the Arrow project at its outset was to define an open standard for column oriented tables, so data frames, that's language independent.
This thorny problem of JVM interop between the JVM world and the native code world is essentially solved by the tools that we've developed in this project, assuming your data is tabular.

I think given how difficult it is to produce open source software, the more that we can defragment the ecosystem and work together to build reusable libraries and portable systems, it will make all of us a great deal more productive and successful.

If you think about open source software as a public good in the sense that we have clean water and roads and sewers, maybe we should take the same approach to essentially individuals, companies and governments funding development of open source infrastructure.

The two things I'm most interested in is sharing data without having to copy or serialize and sharing code, so being able to take an algorithm that somebody else wrote and being able to run it on my data without having to rewrite the algorithm.
